'''John Skandalis''' (born 16 June 1976) is an Australian former professional [[rugby league]] footballer who played as a {{rlp|pr}} in the 1990s, 2000s and 2010s.

He played for the [[Western Suburbs Magpies]] and for the [[Wests Tigers]] in two separate spells in the [[National Rugby League]] (NRL). Skandalis also played for the [[Huddersfield Giants]] in the [[Super League]] and [[City Origin Team|City Origin]] between 2002 and 2006.

==Background==
Skandalis was born in [[Sydney]], New South Wales. The Skandalis family home in [[Lightning Ridge]] burnt down when John was six, causing the family to move to [[Ashfield, New South Wales|Ashfield]], and later [[Minto, New South Wales|Minto]].<ref>{{cite book| author=Daniel Lane|title=Forever Brothers|year=2009
|publisher=Dolphin Press|location=[[Lane Cove]]|isbn=978-0-9803578-5-1|pages=154–155}}</ref> Skandalis' mother died when he was in his teens, leaving his father to raise Skandalis and his sisters. He was "taken in" by Josie McGuinness, the mother of [[Kevin McGuinness|Kevin]] and [[Ken McGuinness]], who, he says, "treated him like [a] son."<ref name=SMH02112009>{{cite news|newspaper=[[Sydney Morning Herald]]|title=Once a rebel, Skando finds cause close to his heart|url=http://www.smh.com.au/rugby-league/league-news/once-a-rebel-skando-finds-cause-close-to-his-heart-20091123-j0my.html|access-date=27 November 2009|date=2 November 2009}}</ref>

==Playing career==
Skandalis signed on for the Magpies in the early 1990s after playing out his junior years with the Minto Cobras, and attending Sarah Redfern High School.<ref>{{cite web|title=Remembering Minto|url=http://rememberingminto.org.au/stories/sandrayoungpaulgavin.html|access-date=10 December 2010|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110218042055/http://www.rememberingminto.org.au/stories/sandrayoungpaulgavin.html|archive-date=18 February 2011}}</ref> His first grade début came in round 9 of the 1996 season against the [[Penrith Panthers]] on 17 May. Coach [[Tommy Raudonikis]] said, "He got in a few brawls that night in his first game in first grade and I knew from that moment that this was a player who wasn't going to let anyone down. I knew from then on that if I gave him the opportunity, he was going to be great for me."<ref name=rlp>{{cite magazine| magazine= [[Rugby League Week]] | title=The Skando Factor|author=Cameron Bell | date=26 August 2009}}</ref> He made 10 appearances that season, including the Magpies last ever finals appearance against Cronulla on 6 September.

A regular in the starting team in 1997, Skandalis scored 3 tries from his 20 appearances. He scored his first in the 24 May match against the [[Gold Coast Chargers]], as they won 26–10. With the decline of the Magpies, Skandalis played in 34 games in the next 2 seasons, with only 5 being victories. He later said, "We used to get smashed every week and we didn't have the best team, but to be honest, those years at the start of my career really made me a tougher player and work that much harder. It helped to build up my mental toughness more than anything."<ref name=rlp />

With the merger with the [[Balmain Tigers]], Skandalis was one of the few former Magpies to join [[Wests Tigers]]. He scored their first try in their debut match on 6 February 2000, a 24-all draw against Brisbane at Campbelltown Stadium. Scoring 6 tries from his 24 games in 2000, it was the first of 7 consecutive seasons in which he played at least 22 games.

Skandalis was known for his workhorse attitude and his never say die approach to rugby league and as the years progressed his performances gradually caught the attention of representative selectors. This was seen in both the 2002 and 2004 seasons where Skandalis was dually awarded with his first representative call up for City team in the annual City v. Country fixture. Skandalis was named the Wests Tigers' player of the year in 2002.<ref name="2010 annual">{{cite journal| editor=David Middleton |title=2010 Official Rugby League Annual|year=2010|publisher=News Magazines for the [[National Rugby League]]|location=Alexandria NSW, 2015
|page=181}}</ref>
[[File:John Skandalis (23 April 2005).jpg|thumb|right|Skandalis in 2005]]
Skandalis was a member of the [[Wests Tigers]] team which won the [[2005 NRL Grand Final]] against the Cowboys. He was named in the ''[[Big League]]'' team of the year for the [[2005 NRL season]].<ref name="rl 2009">Middleton, David (Editor); '''''2009 Official Rugby League Annual'''''; published 2010 for the [[National Rugby League]] by News Magazines, Alexandria NSW, 2015</ref>
As NRL Premiers Wests faced [[Super League]] champions [[Bradford Bulls]] in the [[2006 World Club Challenge]]. Skandalis played as a prop forward in the Tigers' 30–10 loss.
Skandalis held the record for the most first grade games for the [[Wests Tigers]] with 185 appearances. In 2012 the record was surpassed by [[Chris Heighington]], though Skandalis had previously played 64 games for [[Western Suburbs Magpies|Wests Magpies]] before the club merged. He was the last original Western Suburbs player remaining at the Wests Tigers club after the merger in 2000.

On 14 June 2006, Skandalis signed a two-year contract to play in the [[Super League]] with the [[Huddersfield Giants]], starting from the 2007 season. In 2008, Skandalis was made vice-captain of the squad, alongside [[Ryan Hudson]]. He led the team out against Wigan when regular captain [[Chris Thorman]] was injured.

Skandalis returned to Australia at the end of the 2008 season following a knee injury, picked up early in the season, which prevented him from participating further. Skandalis took up a post with Wests Tigers Youth Development.<ref>Giants' Skando to go http://www.examiner.co.uk/huddersfield-giants/huddersfield-giants-news/2008/07/24/giants-skando-to-go-86081-21395497</ref>

In 2009, Skandalis was named in the Wests Magpies NSW Cup side, but was recalled to the Wests Tigers team from round 11, after injuries to other players.<ref>{{cite news|author=Steve Jancetic|newspaper=[[Daily Telegraph (Sydney)|The Daily Telegraph]]|location=Sydney|title=John Skandalis answers Tigers' SOS|url=http://www.news.com.au/dailytelegraph/sport/nrl/story/0,26799,25507683-5006066,00.html|access-date=19 May 2009| date=19 May 2009}}</ref> He remained in the first grade team for the rest of the season.
After the end of the 2009 season, Skandalis announced he would be retiring and taking up the position of strength and conditioning coach with the Wests Tigers [[National Youth Competition (rugby league)|NYC]] team.<ref>{{cite news|newspaper=The Sydney Morning Herald|title=Skandalis retires … for good, this time|author=Adrian Proszenko|url=http://www.smh.com.au/rugby-league/league-news/skandalis-retires-8230-for-good-this-time-20091123-j0n0.html|access-date=27 November 2009|date=22 November 2009}}</ref> However, Skandalis returned to play for the Magpies in the middle of the 2010 season, and then, in July, made a shock comeback with the Tigers,<ref>{{cite news|publisher=ABC Grandstand Sport|title=Broncos back to full strength|url=http://www.abc.net.au/news/stories/2010/07/13/2952660.htm?site=sport&section=rugbyleague|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20121111130006/http://www.abc.net.au/news/stories/2010/07/13/2952660.htm?site=sport&section=rugbyleague|url-status=dead|archive-date=11 November 2012|access-date=23 July 2010|date=13 July 2010}}</ref> coach [[Tim Sheens]] saying, "I can't get rid of the guy."<ref>{{cite news|newspaper=The Sydney Morning Herald|title=Not buts about it, veteran prop is champing at bit for another run|author=Glenn Jackson|url=http://www.smh.com.au/rugby-league/league-match-preview/not-buts-about-it-veteran-prop-is-champing-at-bit-for-another-run-20100923-15oyb.html|access-date=28 June 2011|date=24 September 2010}}</ref> Playing another 5 games, he fell just short of making his 250th NRL appearance. He is the oldest player to have played at the club, being 34 years and 73 days old in his last appearance for the club.<ref name="2010 annual" />
The inaugural "Philips Sports Dad of the Year" was awarded to Skandalis in late 2009, narrowly defeating [[Brett Kimmorley]].<ref>{{cite news|work=The Daily Telegraph|location=Australia|title=Skando top of the pops|author=Phil Rothfield and Rebecca Wilson|url=http://www.dailytelegraph.com.au/sport/skando-top-of-the-pops/story-e6freye0-1225769789150|access-date=22 September 2009|date=6 September 2009}}</ref> He donated the money to the [[Starlight Children's Foundation]].
Skandalis has been made a life member of the Western Suburbs club.<ref>{{cite web|title=2009 Pratten Park Reunion|url=http://www.noelkellyskids.info/2009_pratten_park_reunion|access-date=3 May 2010| publisher=Noel Kelly's Kids}}</ref>

Skandalis' Greek heritage made him eligible to represent [[Greece national rugby league team|Greece]], which he did as captain.<ref>[https://archive.today/20120909191929/http://www.rleague.com/db/article.php?id=26728]</ref> He played for Greece in the 2009 Australian Mediterranean Shield.<ref>{{cite news|publisher=Cumberland Courier|title=Making league a world game
|url=http://cumberland-courier.whereilive.com.au/sport/story/making-league-a-world-game|access-date=23 December 2010|date=6 October 2009}}</ref> Greece won the final 34–14 against Italy.<ref>{{cite news|publisher=Canterbury-Bankstown Express|title=Australian Mediterranean Shield GREECE vs. ITALY|url=http://express.whereilive.com.au/photos/gallery/australian-mediterranean-shield-greece-vs-italy|access-date=22 December 2010|date=19 October 2009}}</ref>

==Post-playing career==
Having spent two years with the Wests Tigers' NYC team, in 2012 Skandalis became the club's head conditioner.<ref>{{cite news|publisher=Roar|title=NRL pre-season gets tough for Tigers|author=David Beniuk|url=http://www.theroar.com.au/2011/11/19/nrl-pre-season-gets-tough-for-tigers|access-date=25 January 2012|date=19 November 2011}}</ref> He was removed from the position with the arrival of new coach [[Jason Taylor (rugby league)|Jason Taylor]] in 2014,<ref>{{cite news| work= The Daily Telegraph|location=Sydney | title=Wests Tigers' incredible staff turnover one of the main reasons for the club's lack of success | url=https://www.dailytelegraph.com.au/sport/nrl/teams/tigers/wests-tigers-incredible-staff-turnover-one-of-the-main-reasons-for-the-clubs-lack-of-success/news-story/277d90d0ecb06cf0ab0ecd23c1798721|author=David Riccio}}</ref> but remained in the club in other roles. In 2017, he became an assistant to the Wests Magpies [[Intrust Super Premiership NSW|ISP]] coach, former teammate [[Brett Hodgson]].<ref>{{cite web | work= Wests Tigers | title= Coach and CEO Q&A – Christmas Edition | url= http://www.weststigers.com.au/news/2015/10/07/_2015_season_review_.html | access-date= 28 December 2017 | archive-url= https://web.archive.org/web/20160304044717/http://www.weststigers.com.au/news/2015/10/07/_2015_season_review_.html | archive-date= 4 March 2016 | url-status= dead }}</ref>

Skandalis has been involved in the ''Macquarie Fields Finding Jobs, Changing Lives'' program, helping troubled youth from the [[Macquarie Fields]] area.<ref name=SMH02112009/> He was made an NRL ambassador in 2017.<ref>{{cite web| publisher= National Rugby League | title=2017 NRL Ambassadors announced | date=25 January 2017 | url=https://www.nrl.com/news/2017/01/25/2017-nrl-ambassadors-announced/}}</ref>
